title: Kotlin Flow in Practice: Streams, State, and Reactive Patterns in Android
excerpt: A practical guide to understanding Kotlin Flow, its core features, trade-offs, and how it compares to other reactive solutions in Android development.
author: Fauzan
content:
Introduction

Handling asynchronous data is a core part of Android development. Whether it is API calls, database updates, or user interactions, applications constantly deal with data that changes over time.

Kotlin Flow provides a structured way to represent and process these changes using streams. It is built on top of coroutines and integrates naturally with modern Android development.

This article explores how Kotlin Flow works, its key features, and how it compares to other approaches.

What is Kotlin Flow?

Kotlin Flow is a type that represents a stream of values that are produced asynchronously. Unlike a simple suspend function that returns a single value, Flow can emit multiple values over time.

val flow = flow {
  emit(1)
  emit(2)
}

A Flow does nothing until it is collected, which makes it predictable and easy to control.

Cold vs Hot Streams

Cold Flow

A cold flow starts executing only when it is collected.

val flow = flow {
  println("Start")
  emit(1)
}

Each collector triggers a new execution.

Hot Flow

Hot flows emit values regardless of collectors. In Kotlin Flow, these are represented by StateFlow and SharedFlow.

  • StateFlow: Holds the latest value
  • SharedFlow: Broadcasts values to multiple collectors

Core Operators

Flow provides a rich set of operators to transform and control data streams.

repository.getUsers()
  .map { it.filter { user -> user.isActive } }
  .debounce(300)
  .distinctUntilChanged()
  .catch { emit(emptyList()) }
  .flowOn(Dispatchers.IO)

Common Operators

  • map: Transform data
  • filter: Filter emissions
  • debounce: Limit rapid emissions
  • combine: Merge multiple flows
  • flatMapLatest: Switch to latest stream

State Management with StateFlow

StateFlow is commonly used to represent UI state.

private val _state = MutableStateFlow(UiState.Loading)
val state: StateFlow<UiState> = _state

It always holds a value and emits updates to collectors.

Event Handling with SharedFlow

SharedFlow is designed for one-time events.

private val _event = MutableSharedFlow<UiEvent>()
val event = _event.asSharedFlow()

This is useful for navigation or showing messages.

Flow Lifecycle Awareness

Flows should be collected with lifecycle awareness to avoid leaks.

lifecycleScope.launch {
  repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) {
    viewModel.state.collect {
      render(it)
    }
  }
}

Error Handling

Error handling is built into Flow using operators.

.catch { e ->
  emit(UiState.Error(e.message ?: "Unknown"))
}

This keeps error logic separate from UI logic.

Backpressure Handling

Flow naturally supports backpressure using suspension. When the collector is slow, the producer suspends instead of overwhelming the system.

This is different from some reactive libraries that require explicit strategies.

Comparison with Other Approaches

Flow vs LiveData

  • Flow is more flexible and supports operators
  • LiveData is lifecycle-aware by default
  • Flow requires manual lifecycle handling

Flow vs RxJava

  • Flow is simpler and integrates with coroutines
  • RxJava has more operators and mature ecosystem
  • Flow has less boilerplate

Flow vs Callbacks

  • Flow is easier to compose
  • Callbacks can become hard to manage at scale

Advantages of Kotlin Flow

  • Seamless coroutine integration
  • Structured concurrency
  • Readable and declarative
  • Built-in backpressure handling

Limitations

  • Requires understanding of coroutines
  • Not lifecycle-aware by default
  • Smaller ecosystem compared to RxJava

Common Pitfalls

  • Collecting flow without lifecycle awareness
  • Using StateFlow for events
  • Forgetting to handle errors
  • Overusing operators unnecessarily

Conclusion

Kotlin Flow provides a solid foundation for handling asynchronous data in Android. It offers a balance between simplicity and flexibility, making it suitable for a wide range of use cases.

Understanding how to use Flow effectively can significantly improve how data is managed across your application.
